topic: Coordinate Collapse — EXP-149 inserted empirical-fitting defense paragraph into Other Side section
change_summary: EXP-149 added one new paragraph inside the SEC-1.8 'Other side' block, immediately after the wavelength-selectivity rebuttal and before the future-ray-trace rebuttal. It preempts the strongest philosophical defense (n(r) as legitimate empirical fit, analogous to Newton fitting G) and rebuts it by pointing to the solar-diameter out-of-sample failure in Section 2.4.
our_argument_summary: The new paragraph concedes that empirical fitting is legitimate science (citing Newton fitting G to observations), then argues that a fitted function is only valid if it predicts beyond its calibration domain. It asserts n(r) was calibrated solely against Polaris elevation angles and that the very first out-of-sample test — applying the same n(r) to solar observations — produces a 52% diurnal angular diameter variation vs. the observed 0.01%, a factor-of-5,200 overshoot. The claim is that n(r) is therefore an overfit to one observable, not a physical law.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The paragraph does not introduce new code-derived numbers — it reuses Section 2.4's geometric calculation (sun-at-5,733km → line-of-sight-distance doubles at 90° hour angle → angular diameter inversely proportional to distance → 52% reduction) and contrasts it with observational constancy (0.01%).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The 52% and 0.01% figures are computed/empirical, not hardcoded display values. They live in Section 2.4 and are cross-referenced here. The dome has no code that computes solar angular diameter through n(r) — Section 2.4's 'refraction escape hatch' paragraph explicitly notes 'No formula or mechanism is specified.'
data_sources_used: Cross-reference to Section 2.4 (SDO/HMI, Royal Observatory Greenwich 1836–1953, micrometer measurements)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None introduced by the new paragraph. The paragraph closes off rather than opens the 'empirical fitting' defense.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None in the new paragraph itself; see hole #1 for the internal numerical inconsistency with the cross-referenced section.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: SEC-1.8 is a structural prose section, not a WIN, so the monitoring tag does not apply in the normal sense. monitor.py has no n(r) / solar-diameter comparison logic.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The section attacks the dome's geometry, does not relabel standard physics.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Not applicable — section is an analytical argument, not a claimed prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Uses dome's own n(r) formula and H(r) function to derive the contradiction.

holes_found.description: Numerical inconsistency with the section it cross-references. The new paragraph ends with 'it fails by a factor of 5,200.' The cross-referenced Section 2.4 concludes 'The prediction fails by a factor of 5,000.' Both numbers are derivable from 52/0.01 = 5,200 (the new paragraph is more precise) but a smart dome defender can quote both lines side-by-side and ask 'which is it?' This is the textbook nitpick the dome defender will reach for — an internal inconsistency that looks like loose arithmetic. Either both sections should use 5,200 (exact), or both should use 'over 5,000' (round), or the paragraph should say 'roughly 5,000×' to match 2.4.
holes_found.recommendation: Harmonize the two sections. Preferred fix: update Section 2.4 from 'a factor of 5,000' to 'a factor of 5,200' (the exact ratio) and keep the new paragraph's 5,200. Alternative: soften the new paragraph to 'over 5,000×' or 'more than 5,000×' to match 2.4's rounded figure. Track as a small patch to sections.json rather than rewriting the EXP-149 paragraph.

holes_found.description: The punch line 'fails by a factor of 5,200' over-reaches relative to what the paragraph itself derives. Strictly: Section 2.4's 52% figure is the pure-geometric prediction with NO refraction compensation. The 52% is what you get when you ignore n(r) and just apply trigonometry to a local sun. The dome's refraction fix is SUPPOSED to compensate that 52% down to 0.01%. The new paragraph asserts that n(r) 'produces' the 52% — but no one (not us, not the dome author) has actually computed what n(r) produces for solar rays. Section 2.4's own text concedes: 'No formula or mechanism is specified.' A dome defender can fairly respond: 'You claim my n(r) produces 52% variation, but you haven't done the 3D ray-trace either. You're attacking a calculation you haven't performed.' The rigorous argument is the earlier 3D-path-integral paragraph, which shows that ANY n(r) strong enough to produce the required sun displacement also produces geometry-dependent stellar shifts incompatible with Gaia. The EXP-149 paragraph skips this chain and jumps to 5,200× as if it were a computed result rather than a bounding assertion.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a half-sentence that makes the logical step explicit. E.g., change 'A local sun at 5,733 km observed through n(r) produces 52% angular diameter variation across the day' to 'A local sun at 5,733 km produces a geometric 52% angular diameter variation across the day; for n(r) to save this prediction it would need to compensate the variation to 0.01% at every zenith angle, all seasons, all latitudes — a functional form n(r) was never fitted to produce.' This keeps the 5,200× punch line but makes the logical step transparent and closes the 'you didn't do the calc' objection.

holes_found.description: The Newton-fitted-G analogy is not quite historically accurate. Newton did not fit G; the Principia does not contain a numerical value for G. G was first measured by Cavendish in 1798 (71 years after Newton's death). What Newton fitted was the FORM of the inverse-square law, calibrated via Kepler's third law and the Moon-test — relative orbital mechanics, not an absolute constant. A defender who knows history of physics will jump on this to discredit the kernel-of-truth concession. The point the paragraph wants to make — that empirical calibration is legitimate science — survives with a better analogy (e.g., Kepler fitting orbital parameters, Cavendish measuring G, or simply 'parameters in physical laws are routinely calibrated from observations').
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Newton fitted G to observations before predicting orbits' with 'Kepler fitted orbital parameters to observations before Newton derived the underlying law' or 'physical constants like G (Cavendish, 1798) are routinely fitted to observations before being used to predict other phenomena.' Minor rewrite; preserves the kernel-of-truth beat.

holes_found.description: Semi-technical misuse of 'overfitting'. In statistics, overfitting means fitting more parameters than the data-generating process warrants, such that the model captures noise. n(r) is a minimal, two-parameter exponential (coefficient 0.20, scale constant 8,537) — it is essentially the opposite of overparameterized. The dome's problem isn't overfitting in the technical sense; it's single-observable calibration (the model's parameters were chosen to satisfy one class of observations — Polaris elevations — with no cross-check against other observables). A statistically literate defender will catch 'overfitting' as loose usage. Severity is minor because the paragraph also uses the precise phrase 'single-observable overfit' which preserves meaning even if the term is imperfect.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider 'single-observable calibration' or 'underdetermined calibration' instead of 'overfitting' in one spot. Not critical — most readers won't notice. Keep if stylistic punch matters more than technical purity.

holes_found.description: TLDR drift check: the SEC-1.8 ps-tldr summary does not mention the solar-diameter / 52% failure at all — it focuses on the coordinate-contamination argument and the 300–6,000× refraction ratio. The EXP-149 paragraph introduces a NEW analytical beat (n(r) is an overfit, the solar diameter is the smoking gun) that is arguably the strongest anti-defense argument in the whole section. A reader who reads only the TLDR and the summary-table cell would miss this line of attack entirely. This is not a TLDR factual error (still minor severity per curmudgeon rules) but it is a weakening of coverage.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: add one sentence to the TLDR along the lines of 'Applying the same refraction fix to solar observations predicts 52% diurnal angular-diameter variation — falsified by 190 years of 0.01% constancy.' Two-sentence TLDR convention remains intact and the reader sees the self-consistency kill-shot up front.

stronger_arguments.description: The paragraph could explicitly invoke the 'no-free-lunch' asymmetry: n(r) has ONE free scalar function; the dome needs it to simultaneously satisfy (a) Polaris elevation = latitude at all r, (b) solar angular diameter constant to 0.01%, (c) Gaia multi-wavelength astrometry to microarcsec precision, and (d) internal consistency with the H(r) function it was fit against. Four independent constraints on one function — almost certainly overdetermined. This is a stronger statement of the 'empirical fit' objection than the EXP-149 paragraph currently makes.
sources: Internal — combine SEC-1.8 3D-ray-trace paragraph with Section 2.4 solar diameter with WIN-016 Gaia astrometry

stronger_arguments.description: An even sharper version: solar angular diameter constancy can be quantified against chord length. Zenith-angle changes from 0° to 90° during the day; at fixed distance, 1/cos(zenith angle) factor for magnification is what's required to compensate. If n(r) provides any magnification at all, it is radius-dependent (function of r), not zenith-dependent (function of observation angle). Therefore n(r) CANNOT compensate solar angular diameter variation across the day from a SINGLE observation point at fixed r — the required compensation is angle-dependent but the available correction is radial-only. This is a short, one-line proof that the entire refraction defense is structurally incompatible with the solar-diameter observation.
sources: Mathematical argument from n(r) form — derivable, not published elsewhere

kernel_of_truth.description: The paragraph correctly identifies that empirical fitting of a scalar function to one observable is a normal move in physics and IS how many laws get calibrated. The EXP-149 kernel_of_truth field in the source expansion says essentially this and frames the problem as the out-of-sample failure, not the method. That framing is fair and strengthens the credibility of the critique.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Legitimate empirical fits must survive out-of-sample tests. n(r) was calibrated on Polaris elevations and the first independent application (solar angular diameter) produces a discrepancy of 3+ orders of magnitude from observation. More importantly — and this is the structural point that should be made more explicit — a radial-only function cannot supply zenith-angle-dependent compensation, so it cannot save solar diameter constancy regardless of how it is parameterized.

deeper_analysis:
Overall assessment: the EXP-149 insertion is a net-positive addition to SEC-1.8. It closes a genuine gap — the 'empirical fitting is legitimate' defense was the strongest philosophical counter and was not addressed by the earlier rebuttal paragraphs (which focused on chromatic refraction and wavelength selectivity). The paragraph's structure — concede the method, deny the out-of-sample survival — mirrors how Popperian falsification is supposed to work, which gives it rhetorical force. The three issues I flag are all survivable: the 5,200 vs 5,000 inconsistency is a 5-minute patch, the 'n(r) produces 52%' framing is a one-sentence clarification, and the Newton-G analogy is a word-substitution. None undermines the core argument, and the core argument is correct: a single-parameter radial function cannot simultaneously satisfy the Polaris, solar, and Gaia constraints. 

The strongest improvement available is to add the 'zenith-angle vs radial function' observation (see stronger_arguments item 2) — this converts the current 'out-of-sample failure' argument (which relies on a calculation nobody has done) into a structural impossibility proof (which needs no calculation because it's a functional-form mismatch). That single sentence would turn this paragraph from rhetorically strong to logically airtight. 

I recommend the decider accept the paragraph with the 5,200→harmonize patch and the 'n(r) produces vs geometric produces' clarification. The Newton-G fix is optional; the TLDR update is optional. No verdict change required.

Tab 'selftest' is the correct tab for part2 (verified part2.tab = 'selftest')

Section 2.4's 0.01% daily constancy claim is present and sourced to SDO/HMI + Royal Observatory Greenwich (verified in cited section prose)
n(r) formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8,537/H(r) − 1) is consistent with the formula used in earlier SEC-1.8 paragraphs (verified)
citations_failed: Section 2.4 states 'The prediction fails by a factor of 5,000' while EXP-149 paragraph states 'fails by a factor of 5,200' — both internally derivable from 52/0.01=5,200 but inconsistent with each other when quoted verbatim. This is not a fabricated citation but a numerical rounding divergence between cross-referenced sections.
citations_unchecked: Newton-fitted-G historical claim (questionable as stated; flagged as minor hole)
'Micrometer measurements since the 1830s (Royal Observatory Greenwich, 1836–1953)' — inherited from Section 2.4, not re-verified here
